1.synthesis: Now concerning spiritual gifts, Paul does not want them to be uninformed. They know that when they were pagans they were led astray to mute idols—so he wants them to understand that no one speaking by the Spirit of God says 'Jesus is accursed!' and no one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:1–3). There are varieties of gifts but the same Spirit; varieties of service but the same Lord; varieties of activities but the same God who empowers them all in everyone (1 Corinthians 12:4–6). To each is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common good: wisdom, knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, discernment, tongues, interpretation (1 Corinthians 12:7–10). All these are empowered by one and the same Spirit, who apportions to each one individually as he wills (1 Corinthians 12:11).
1.historical_context: The Corinthian problem with spiritual gifts centered on tongues, which some in the community had elevated to the supreme spiritual gift. The pre-conversion context of ecstatic idol-worship (v.2) is significant: the Corinthians may have carried over pagan expectations of ecstatic experience as the mark of divine encounter, and applied them to the Christian charismata. The christological discernment principle (vv.2–3) provides the foundational test: not the intensity or impressiveness of the spiritual experience but its confession of Jesus as Lord determines its Spirit-origin. The threefold repetition of 'same Spirit / same Lord / same God' (vv.4–6) grounds the diversity of gifts in the unity of the triune God, anticipating the body-of-Christ unity argument of verses 12–31.
1.literary_structure: The passage moves from discernment criterion (vv.1–3) to the unity-in-diversity principle (vv.4–6) to the gift-list (vv.7–10) to the Spirit's sovereign distribution (v.11). The gift-list is not exhaustive (cf. Romans 12:6–8; Ephesians 4:11) but representative, with tongues placed last—a deliberate ordering in a community that has elevated tongues to first. The 'for the common good' (v.7) is the purpose clause that governs the entire list: every gift is given not for individual spiritual satisfaction but for the building up of the community.
1.theological_application: The 'for the common good' purpose of every spiritual gift (v.7) is the corrective to every individualistic spirituality that treats gifts as personal spiritual achievements or experiences. No gift belongs to the individual who exercises it—it is distributed by the Spirit, owned by the community, and evaluated by its contribution to communal upbuilding. The Spirit's sovereign apportionment ('as he wills,' v.11) eliminates the ground for both pride (I achieved this gift) and shame (I don't have the impressive gift). The church's stewardship of spiritual gifts must hold together the individual's responsibility to exercise their specific gift and the community's responsibility to create space for every gift to contribute. The trinitarian grounding of gift-diversity (vv.4–6) points toward the deepest rationale: the triune God whose own life contains diversity within unity pours out that same pattern into the community that bears his name.
1.christology: 'No one can say Jesus is Lord except by the Holy Spirit' (v.3) is the christological discernment criterion for all spiritual gifts: the Spirit's work always confesses Jesus as Lord. This is the trinitarian test: the Spirit points to the Son who reveals the Father. Every spiritual gift, when genuinely from the Holy Spirit, serves the exaltation of the risen Christ and the building of the community that bears his name. The Lord Jesus Christ is the 'same Lord' of verse 5—the one in whose service all the gifts are exercised, whose own ministry during his earthly life exemplified wisdom, knowledge, healing, miracles, prophecy, and discernment, and who now distributes these same capacities through his Spirit to the many members of his body.

12.synthesis: As the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one body—so it is with Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12). For in one Spirit they were all baptized into one body—Jews or Greeks, slaves or free—and all were made to drink of one Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:13). The body does not consist of one member but of many. If the foot says 'I am not a hand, therefore I do not belong to the body'—it does not cease to be part of the body (1 Corinthians 12:14–15). If the whole body were an eye, where would be the sense of hearing? God arranged the members, each one of them, as he chose (1 Corinthians 12:17–18). The eye cannot say to the hand, 'I have no need of you'—nor can the head say it to the feet (1 Corinthians 12:21). On the contrary, the parts of the body that seem weaker are indispensable, and those we think less honorable we treat with greater honor (1 Corinthians 12:22–23). God has so composed the body that there may be no division in it, but that the members may have the same care for one another. If one member suffers, all suffer together; if one member is honored, all rejoice together (1 Corinthians 12:25–26).
12.historical_context: The body politic metaphor was a well-known political device in the Greco-Roman world (Livy records a version attributed to Menenius Agrippa about the Roman plebeians). Paul's use of it differs in crucial ways: in the political version, the metaphor typically justifies the existing social hierarchy; in Paul's version, the weaker and seemingly less honorable members receive greater honor (vv.22–24). The baptism-into-one-body formula (v.13) abolishes the social distinctions (Jew/Greek, slave/free) that the Corinthian community was reproducing through its spiritual hierarchy. The 'so it is with Christ' (v.12)—not 'so it is with the church'—identifies the body directly with Christ: the community is not merely Christ-like but is his body in the world.
12.literary_structure: The body metaphor develops in two directions: against the member who considers itself not belonging because it differs from others (vv.14–20—the foot/ear problem, which addresses those who feel inadequate without the prestigious gifts) and against the member who considers itself not needing others (vv.21–26—the eye/hand problem, which addresses those who have prestigious gifts and dismiss those without them). Both forms of dysfunction undermine the body's unity. The co-suffering/co-rejoicing principle (v.26) is the communal goal that the diverse body is designed to achieve.
12.theological_application: The body-of-Christ ecclesiology is one of Paul's most powerful theological contributions. The body's diversity (many members) serves its unity (one body), and neither member that feels inadequate nor member that feels superior is right about their distinctiveness. The Corinthian problem of over-valuing tongues is addressed by the insistence that God arranged every member as he chose (v.18)—the distribution is God's decision, not the Spirit-world's achievement hierarchy. The weaker members' indispensability (v.22) and the less honorable members' greater honor (v.23) deliberately invert the status hierarchy: those at the bottom of the Corinthian spiritual prestige ladder are precisely those toward whom the community should direct its greatest care. Co-suffering and co-rejoicing (v.26) describe the empathic solidarity that body-belonging produces: the community that truly functions as Christ's body cannot be indifferent to any member's pain or excluded from any member's joy.
12.christology: 'So it is with Christ' (v.12)—the body and Christ are identified: Christ is not merely head of the body but constitutive of it. The baptism into one body (v.13) is baptism into Christ himself, which means the community that has been so baptized is the ongoing form of Christ's presence in the world. The diversity of members reflects the comprehensive scope of Christ's own ministry: in his earthly life he exercised wisdom, healing, prophecy, and service; in his body the church, these same capacities are distributed among its many members so that together they can do what he alone did in his earthly ministry. The body metaphor is therefore not merely sociological but christological: the church is the bodily presence of the risen Christ in the world, with all the implications that follow for how each member treats every other.

27.synthesis: Now they are the body of Christ and individually members of it. God has appointed in the church first apostles, second prophets, third teachers, then miracles, then gifts of healing, helping, administrating, various kinds of tongues (1 Corinthians 12:27–28). Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Do all work miracles? Do all possess gifts of healing? Do all speak with tongues? Do all interpret? (1 Corinthians 12:29–30.) But they should earnestly desire the higher gifts. And he will show them a still more excellent way (1 Corinthians 12:31).
27.historical_context: The ordering of roles (apostles, prophets, teachers first; tongues last) in verse 28 deliberately de-prioritizes tongues, which the Corinthians had elevated to first. Apostles as first reflects their foundational role in the church (cf. Ephesians 2:20); prophets and teachers follow as the primary ministries of the word. The rhetorical questions of verses 29–30 (all apostles? all speak tongues?) expect the answer 'no' and demonstrate the body's diversity—not everyone has the same gift, including tongues. The 'still more excellent way' of verse 31 sets up chapter 13: love is not itself a charism but the mode in which all charisms are to be exercised, and it surpasses them all because it belongs to the permanent order (13:13) that charisms serve only provisionally.
27.literary_structure: The passage concludes the body-metaphor section with the explicit application ('you are the body of Christ,' v.27) and the role-listing that integrates the metaphor's abstract principle with the community's specific ordered ministries. The rhetorical questions (vv.29–30) demonstrate that gift-diversity is God's design rather than an aberration. The transition in verse 31 (earnestly desire the higher gifts; but I will show you a still more excellent way) is the hinge between the gift-theology of chapters 12 and 14 and the love-theology of chapter 13: love is the way in which gifts are to be pursued and exercised, and it is more excellent than any specific gift.
27.theological_application: The ordering of gifts with apostles first and tongues last (v.28) has permanent relevance for the church's self-organization: the ministries that provide foundational proclamation and teaching (apostles, prophets, teachers) are most important for the community's health, while the more spectacular manifestations (tongues, miracles) are placed lower in the ordering. This does not make them unimportant, but it prevents the Corinthian inversion that placed display-gifts above teaching-gifts. The earnest desire for the 'higher gifts' (v.31) is not a command to covet what one does not have but an exhortation to value the gifts that build up the community most effectively. The entire gift-framework of chapters 12–14 must be held together: diversity (ch.12), love as the governing mode (ch.13), and the ordering of gifts by community-building criteria (ch.14).
27.christology: God has 'appointed' (tithēmi, v.28) the gifts and roles in the church—the distribution is a divine act of arrangement, not human achievement or charismatic competition. The risen Christ who received from the Father and poured out the Spirit (Acts 2:33) is the one who distributes the gifts to the members of his body. The 'more excellent way' of verse 31 is love—and Christ himself is the ultimate exemplar and source of that love: 'God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). Chapter 13's hymn to love is ultimately a description of the love that moved the Son to give himself, now poured into the hearts of believers through the Spirit who is also the gift-giver.
